# Transcrição: Metaphors

- **Fonte:** Metaphors
- **Duração:** 07:58
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hey guys, this is Mark. Welcome to Study Buddy Session Time. Great to have you guys here. Today we are going to be talking about metaphors. So let's talk about metaphors. Metaphors are like a word, as we probably already all know, metaphors are like a word picture that we use to communicate, and we use specifically to communicate complex situations and strong emotions. So very often people will – you will find them in – when you find metaphors in their natural habitat as it were, it will be where a client is talking about something that's important to them, where they may be frustrated, where they may feel like they are a little helpless, and they will express a situation in metaphorical terms. So let's say something like, I'm stuck. I'm being blocked. I can't move. I can't get past this. I can't get over this. It's dragging me around. I'm dragging it around. I'm crowded. These are all spatial metaphors that people use to express situations, and they use it because it's kind of a highly emotional shorthand for talking about what's going on. Most of the time when someone expresses something very emotional, it's metaphorical in nature. So even words that we use like overwhelmed, like overwhelmed is a metaphor. Overwhelmed means that something is kind of flooding over you, and you are basically sinking into something. Now, as coaches, you are going to find situations where people bring up a metaphor, and when you do, you have an opportunity because the person has just expressed a highly emotional truth to you. And in a format that is because it's an imaginary word picture, it is available to be changed. So if someone says, stuck, I'm feeling stuck, you can say, let's get you unstuck. Let's get you unglued. What would it like to be unstuck? What would it be like to move away from the stuckness? Things like that. That's a metaphorical way that you've accepted the way that the person has expressed themselves, and because it's a word picture, you can play with it and offer them alternatives. Now, today we are going to talk about since we are all coaches here, and so we are not clients in the heat of the moment expressing something in a metaphor. So let me tell you one more thing about that. Metaphors work because they create an opening. The person has just expressed themselves. You have an ability to modify it, tweak it, transform it based on the metaphor. And that opens up an emotional opening because where they were just frustrated, where they were just angry or upset, they are moved into a place of reimagining

## [03:01 - 06:02]

how it can be, and that creates an emotional opening for them to actually make stronger, smarter, more informed decisions. So today we are going to do metaphors with each other in practice session. And let me give you what to do, and then we will talk about the format for the call. So for anyone who is new, this is our practice sessions. We are going to pair off using this maestro conference. So in a couple of minutes you will be paired off with another student. You are going to do an exercise together. One is going to be the coach, one is going to be the client. We are going to take 30 minutes for you to do this together. And then after 30 minutes, Ryan will get on and tell you that it is time to switch. So you will switch who is the client and who is the coach. You will do the exercise for 30 minutes. And then we will regroup in the main room for sharing. We would love to hear about any breakthroughs and thoughts that you have. So what we are going to do today is when you come in, you are going to decide who is going to be the coach and who is going to be the client with you and your partner. And sometimes we may have three people in a room if we have an odd number, so we can make that work. So if you are a coach, ask a question. One way that you can ask a question is to say something like, tell me about something that is important in your life, or let's talk about something that is where you could use some help, or you can ask more directly, are there any metaphors that you find yourself repeating to yourself, or that you are thinking about when you are trying to work on something that you care about? So ask the client for the metaphor. And now what you will do is you will take that metaphor and you will work with the client to see if they can find another way of using that metaphor. What would be some ways of transforming that metaphor? So for instance, if the person says, I feel overwhelmed, right? Or I am swamped. Swamped is a good one. People very typically say, I am swamped. So swamped is a metaphor, right? It means you are stuck in a swamp. You have got gunky, muddy stuff all over you. It's hard to walk. It's hard to get leverage. It's hard to get out. So what are some metaphorical ways of transforming being swamped? You are swamped, well let me throw you a rope. You are swamped, well let's get a ladder. How do we get you out of that swamp? What would be better than a swamp? Let me air lift you out of that swamp and now where can I drop you off? Using my helicopter. These are playful ways of talking about emotional expressions. So I want you guys both, both clients and coach, to use your imaginations

## [06:02 - 07:58]

in different ways of transforming that metaphor so that you get well-practiced with this as a coach. When you are well-practiced with this as a coach, you will be working with a client that will express the metaphor. You will repeat it. You will transform it. No one has ever been informed that you are doing it and it makes the client feel better. Another way to think about metaphor by the way guys, is that sometimes people, when people are uncomfortable with an idea or a decision, they will express a metaphor. I don't get it. It's too hard. It's not comfortable. These are all kind of metaphorical if you think about it. So you can use a metaphor to express something that's more comfortable or that's familiar to them. So for instance, let's imagine that your client is a teacher and they are having to make some financial changes to their life and they are unhappy with it and they don't understand how they are going to for instance live in a budget. This is kind of a stupid example but let's just go with it. They don't know how to do it. So you can bring a metaphorical analogy and say, well you know what, when you have kids in your classroom, sometimes there will be situations when, dot, dot, dot, and you will bring a metaphorical analogy to something that they already know. So that's another way to use a metaphor. So we've talked about three different ways. One is that someone brings up a metaphorical expression spontaneously and you find ways to transform it. Secondly, when someone brings up a metaphor that is holding them back or some way that they are expressing something important in their life and you can find ways to transform it. And the third is to actually use a metaphor to connect someone with something with which they are familiar and comfortable.
